primary_diagnosis.name: Severe seborrhoeic dermatitis with acute anterior blepharitis (acute exacerbation)

differential.evidence_basis: Greasy yellow scale on erythematous skin in the classic seborrhoeic distribution (scalp, eyebrows, nasolabial folds, central chest) on a chronic dandruff background, with lid-margin crusting, is textbook seborrhoeic dermatitis with associated anterior blepharitis.

The telephone-triage framing raised acute periocular infection; it is a genuine must-not-miss around the eye and must be actively screened (fever, hot tensely-swollen spreading erythema, painful/restricted eye movements, proptosis) rather than assumed away.

Acute weeping crusting facial eruption near the eye raises herpetic superinfection; screened by looking for grouped vesicles, monomorphic punched-out erosions, systemic toxicity, true eye pain, photophobia or vision change.
Considered for scaly erythematous plaques, but psoriasis gives thick dry silvery-white scale and does not favour the nasolabial folds and eyelids the way this greasy-scale seborrhoeic pattern does.
Considered for central facial erythema, but rosacea lacks greasy scale and typically spares the nasolabial folds, which are heavily involved here.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Early on, periorbital cellulitis and herpetic eye involvement are legitimate must-not-miss diagnoses; the reasoning task is to screen the sight-threat and systemic red flags (true eye pain, photophobia, vision change, fever, spreading hot swelling, vesicles/punched-out erosions) rather than anchor on 'benign dandruff' and miss an eye emergency, OR anchor on 'infection near the eye' and over-escalate to ED/antibiotics.

differential.evidence_basis: Negative sight-threat screen (no deep eye pain, no photophobia, no vision change - DI-004), no systemic upset and no spreading hot periorbital swelling (DI-005), and no vesicles or punched-out erosions (DI-006) together confirm the benign greasy-scale eruption with lid-margin crusting.

Not absolutely excludable remotely (telehealth cannot examine the eye or measure temperature), but a negative fever / spreading-swelling / eye-movement screen makes it unlikely; retained as a safety-net target.
No grouped vesicles, no monomorphic punched-out erosions, no systemic toxicity, no true eye pain/photophobia/vision change; greasy scale rather than vesicular morphology argues strongly against it.

differential_progression.scoring_note: A negative sight-threat and systemic red-flag screen flips the leading diagnosis firmly to benign seborrhoeic dermatitis/blepharitis and removes any indication for antibiotics or antivirals - but an explicit, mandatory eye safety-net remains required because the eye cannot be examined remotely.

differential.evidence_basis: Benign, common, chronic-relapsing yeast-driven eruption with associated anterior lid-margin blepharitis; management is antifungal shampoo to scalp/face, a short course of mild topical steroid to the facial plaques, lid hygiene, reassurance and an eye safety-net - not antibiotics, antivirals or ED escalation.

differential_progression.scoring_note: An explicit, mandatory eye safety-net (vision change, true eye pain/photophobia, spreading hot periorbital swelling, fever -> urgent same-day in-person / ED) is required even though the leading diagnosis is benign and telehealth-manageable.

red_flags.description: True deep eye pain, photophobia, or reduced/blurred vision - would indicate corneal / herpetic keratitis or other sight-threatening ocular involvement
red_flags.clinical_significance: Sight-threatening keratitis (especially herpetic) requires urgent same-day ophthalmology; topical steroids to the eye can worsen herpetic keratitis and must be avoided.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated herpetic/corneal keratitis can cause corneal scarring and permanent visual loss.

red_flags.description: Fever or systemic illness, or hot tensely-swollen rapidly-spreading periorbital/facial erythema, proptosis or painful/restricted eye movements - periorbital progressing to orbital cellulitis
red_flags.clinical_significance: Orbital cellulitis is a sight- and life-threatening emergency needing urgent IV antibiotics and imaging; periorbital cellulitis needs same-day in-person assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progression to orbital cellulitis, cavernous sinus thrombosis, vision loss or intracranial spread.

red_flags.description: Grouped clear vesicles, monomorphic punched-out erosions or systemic toxicity - eczema herpeticum (HSV superinfection)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Eczema herpeticum is a dermatological emergency requiring systemic aciclovir and, with ocular involvement, urgent ophthalmology; topical steroids can worsen it.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Disseminated HSV, keratitis and, rarely, life-threatening viraemia if mislabelled as benign dermatitis and treated with steroid alone.

pathophysiology_summary: Seborrhoeic dermatitis is a common, benign, chronic-relapsing inflammatory eruption driven by an exuberant host response to commensal Malassezia yeast in sebum-rich skin, producing greasy yellow scale on erythematous skin in a characteristic distribution: scalp, eyebrows, glabella, nasolabial folds, external ears and central chest. Flares are precipitated by psychological stress and cold, dry weather (both present here). Involvement of the eyelid margins produces anterior (seborrhoeic) blepharitis - greasy crusting at the lash bases causing lid-margin burning and the eyes gluing shut overnight, with at most mild reactive conjunctival injection. The clinical challenge in this case is that an explosive periocular flare mimics acute periocular infection: periorbital/preseptal cellulitis, herpetic keratitis and eczema herpeticum are the must-not-miss mimics the telephone triage correctly flagged. They are distinguished by true deep eye pain, photophobia, reduced vision, fever/systemic illness, hot tensely-swollen rapidly-spreading periorbital erythema with proptosis or restricted painful eye movements, and grouped vesicles or monomorphic punched-out erosions - all screened negative here. Management of the benign disease is antifungal (ketoconazole 2% shampoo) to reduce Malassezia load on scalp and face, a short course of mild topical corticosteroid to calm the facial inflammation, and mechanical lid hygiene to debride the crusts; it controls but does not cure a lifelong relapsing condition.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Over-triage to ED / over-treatment with antibiotics
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being driven by the alarming periocular presentation and severe distress to escalate an afebrile patient with a negative sight-threat screen to the emergency department, or to prescribe systemic antibiotics for what is a benign yeast-driven inflammatory eruption.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Reflexive 'cannot examine the eye so must escalate'
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Using telehealth's inability to examine the eye to floor the tier above what the negative patient-obtainable red-flag screen supports, rather than recognising the benign cause and attaching a mandatory eye safety-net with a lower threshold for an in-person look.

The opposite failure: dismissing the eyelid symptoms as 'just dandruff' and failing to screen for or safety-net the sight-threatening mimics (herpetic keratitis, periorbital/orbital cellulitis, eczema herpeticum).

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Topical steroid on or near the eye
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Applying the facial steroid to the eyelids / into the eye, or continuing it long-term on facial skin - risking steroid-induced skin atrophy, ocular hypertension/glaucoma and worsening of an unrecognised herpetic keratitis.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Correct diagnosis without an eye safety-net
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Recognising benign seborrhoeic dermatitis but failing to give an explicit, mandatory safety-net for the periocular mimics that cannot be excluded remotely.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Slit-lamp / fluorescein assessment of the cornea for herpetic dendritic keratitis
Formal visual-acuity measurement and assessment of conjunctival injection
Assessment of extraocular movements and proptosis for orbital involvement
Palpation and true measured temperature to gauge spreading periorbital cellulitis
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Manage the benign seborrhoeic dermatitis / anterior blepharitis with ketoconazole 2% shampoo to scalp and face, a short (max 5-7 day) course of mild topical hydrocortisone 1% to the facial plaques (kept away from the eyes), and mechanical lid hygiene; because telehealth cannot examine the eye, offer a prompt (within 24-48 h) in-person GP look at the periocular region and give a prominent, mandatory eye safety-net (vision change, true deep eye pain/photophobia, spreading hot periorbital swelling, fever, or grouped vesicles/punched-out erosions -> urgent same-day in-person / ED). Do NOT prescribe systemic antibiotics or antivirals, do NOT put steroid on the eyelids, and do NOT escalate to ED for the benign eruption when the sight-threat screen is negative.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Moderate: the greasy-scale seborrhoeic constellation is characteristic and telehealth-recognisable, but the periocular involvement cannot be examined remotely - so the correct posture is a presumptive benign diagnosis with conservative management PLUS a mandatory eye safety-net and a low threshold for an in-person eye look, avoiding both over-triage (ED/antibiotics) and under-triage (missing a herpetic keratitis or periorbital cellulitis).
